chunk_summary: This section outlines the follow-up plan for infants and children with neurologic presentations of vitamin B12 deficiency, emphasizing early treatment, structured follow-up, and red flags for escalation.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical presentation of vitamin B12 deficiency with neurologic symptoms in infancy and early childhood.
Learn the immediate treatment principles, including prioritization of parenteral B12 therapy.
Identify key components of baseline documentation and structured follow-up for affected children.
Recognize red flags requiring urgent escalation in B12 deficiency cases.
key_facts: Vitamin B12 deficiency in infancy can present with neuroregression, tremor, hypotonia, irritability, seizures, or feeding refusal, often without anemia.
Immediate treatment should prioritize parenteral B12 therapy over oral therapy if neurologic symptoms are present.
Baseline documentation should include milestones, regression timeline, tone, reflexes, head circumference, and feeding patterns.
Early follow-up at 2–4 weeks should assess clinical response and adherence to therapy, while medium follow-up at ~3 months should include developmental screening and hearing assessment.
Red flags include persistent seizures, progressive regression, focal neurologic signs, or failure to improve in alertness/feeding within expected timelines.
reference_quote: Delay in treatment risks incomplete neurologic recovery, so the follow-up plan should be written at the first visit. (IAP standard treatment guidelines: Megaloblastic anemia)
